desc: The server binds to all interfaces with Flask debug mode enabled, exposing the interactive debugger and potential remote code execution.
User creation, modification, deletion, and state reset endpoints require no authentication or authorization.
The unauthenticated /debug/state endpoint exposes all users, audit-log entries, and the last operation result.
The /reset GET endpoint destructively clears application state, allowing accidental activation, CSRF, crawlers, and prefetchers to trigger data loss.
Generating IDs as len(USERS) + 1 reuses an existing ID when a non-final user is deleted and another user is created.
Concurrent requests mutate shared USERS, REQUEST_LOG, and LAST_RESULT without synchronization, causing races such as duplicate IDs and lost or inconsistent state.
Keeping application data in module globals makes state non-durable and inconsistent across restarts or multiple worker processes.
POST and PUT accept arbitrary name and age values, including null and nonnumeric ages, which can later break filtering, sorting, arithmetic, or JSON serialization.
GET /user converts min_age with int() without handling invalid or out-of-range input, producing a server error instead of a client error.
PUT permits a missing age and assigns None to the user, corrupting the record and causing subsequent sorting or filtering requests to fail.
Mixed or invalid age types can raise TypeError in both the min_age comparison and age-based sorting.
/doStuff performs unchecked arithmetic on client-controlled values, so null, strings, collections, and other nonnumeric values can cause unhandled exceptions.
USERS and REQUEST_LOG grow without bounds, allowing sustained requests to exhaust process memory.
LAST_RESULT is global across all clients, so one client's operation result can be exposed to another client through /debug/state.
LAST_RESULT stores references to mutable user objects, so later updates can silently change what is reported as the result of an earlier operation.
